name: Qassimi Arabic

ancestors.notes: Central Najdi base — Qassimi is a Najdi sub-variety of the Qaṣīm region (Buraydah, ʿUnayzah).

notes: Qassimi Arabic (قصيمي), the Najdi variety of the Qaṣīm region of central Saudi Arabia (Buraydah, ʿUnayzah, ar-Rass). A sub-dialect of Najdi; it inherits from ar-SA-x-najd the qaf ق → /ɡ/ reflex, the ض/ظ → [ðˤ] merger with retained interdentals ث /θ/ ذ /ð/, the gahawa-syndrome epenthesis, and the VELAR AFFRICATION /k/ → [ts], /ɡ/ → [dz] in front-vowel environments. Qassimi is in fact THE variety for which the dental-affricate reflex [t͡s]/[d͡z] is the salient regional shibboleth (Al-Rojaie 2013; Alhoody 2019, quoting Al-Rojaie 2013:43: /k/→[t͡s], /ɡ/→[d͡z] before front vowels, [at͡sil] 'food', [briːd͡z] 'teapot') — inherited unchanged from the Najdi parent, which already emits the dental affricates. Al-Rojaie (2013) documents this affrication as RECEDING under dialect levelling toward the [k]/[ɡ] of the pan-Najdi/urban standard, most advanced among younger and more-educated speakers; the transcriber models the traditional affricated reflex (the diagnostic form), and this caveat is honest not silent. DEFINING DELTA modeled here vs central Najdi: MONOPHTHONGIZATION — the Classical diphthongs /aj aw/ are realised [eː oː] (Alhoody 2019:42, exx. /bajt/→[beːt] 'house', /lawn/→[loːn] 'colour'; the diphthong prohibition is attributed there to Ingham 1994 and Watson 2002), giving Qassimi the 8-vowel system /a u i; aː uː iː eː oː/ — where central Najdi (ar-SA-x-najd) retains the diphthongs [aj aw] (بَيْت → [bajt]). Declared as allophone_rules QAS_MONO_AY/AW plus the يَوْ→[joː]/وَيْ→[weː] spelled-out keys, exactly as in the Hejazi spec: the inherited يَ→/ja/ and وَ→/wa/ onset digraphs eat the fatha before an /aj//aw/ atom can form, so ⟨يَوْ⟩ and ⟨وَيْ⟩ are spelled out to reach the same monophthong from the spelling. Also documented but NOT engine-expressible: Qassimi (like Najdi generally) permits initial CC clusters via elision of a short high vowel in an open syllable (Alhoody 2019:43, /ktabat/ 'she wrote', /ktaːb/ 'book', /ɡtˤaʕ/ 'pieces') — o2i transcribes the orthography+harakat as written and does not resyllabify, so *katab→ktab elision is not applied; and medial glottal-stop loss with compensatory lengthening (/raʔs/→[raːs], /ðiʔb/→[ðiːb], Alhoody 2019:41), which is a lexical-stratum fact left to a downstream lexicon. NO ar-SA umbrella node exists: Saudi dialects split across the Najdi, Hejazi, Gulf and Southwestern groups and the literature recognises no pan-Saudi phonological grouping (Ingham 1994; Prochazka 1988), so ar-SA stays unassigned and this leaf hangs off ar-SA-x-najd. INPUT CONTRACT: transcribes orthography+harakat; short vowels surface only when the input is vocalised.

sources.author: Alhoody, M.M.A.
sources.title: Phonological adaptation of English loanwords into Qassimi Arabic: an optimality-theoretic account (PhD thesis)
sources.notes: §3.1.2-3.1.4 the Qassimi consonant and vowel inventories. p.41: qaf /q/→[ɡ] (/qaːl/→[ɡaːl] 'he said'), /q/ retained in CA/MSA borrowings (/qurʔaːn/, /qaːrrah/); CA /dˤ/ lost, ض/ظ a single phoneme /ðˤ/ (/dˤarab/ and /ðˤalaːl/ both begin [ðˤ], citing Ingham 1994:13); medial /ʔ/→compensatory lengthening (/raʔs/→[raːs], /ðiʔb/→[ðiːb]). p.42: velar affrication /k/→[t͡s], /ɡ/→[d͡z] before front vowels ([at͡sil] 'food', [briːd͡z] 'teapot', quoting Al-Rojaie 2013:43); 8-vowel system with /eː oː/ from monophthongized /aj aw/ (/bajt/→[beːt], /lawn/→[loːn]); /a/→[ɑ] adjacent to emphatics ([ðˤɑːʕ] 'he got lost' vs [ðaːʕ] 'he spread'). p.43: initial CC clusters via short-vowel elision (/ktabat/, /ktaːb/, /ɡtˤaʕ/).

sources.author: Al-Rojaie, Y.
sources.title: Regional dialect levelling in Najdi Arabic: The case of the deaffrication of [ts] in the Qaṣīmī dialect
sources.notes: The definitive sociolinguistic study of the Qassimi dental affricate [t͡s] (< /k/) and its levelling toward pan-Najdi [k]. p.43: /k/→[t͡s], /ɡ/→[d͡z] before front vowels. Documents deaffrication as an ongoing change led by younger, educated and female speakers — the affricated reflex is the traditional/diagnostic Qassimi form and is receding, not obsolete.

sources.author: Ingham, B.
sources.title: Najdi Arabic: Central Arabian
sources.notes: The Najdi parent's primary reference: /k/→[ts], /ɡ/→[dz] affrication; gahawa syndrome; ض/ظ merge to [ðˤ]; qaf→/ɡ/. p.13 the consonant inventory Alhoody 2019 builds on for Qassim.

sources.author: Watson, J.C.E.
sources.title: The Phonology and Morphology of Arabic
sources.notes: Cited by Alhoody 2019:42 for the Arabic-general diphthong prohibition underlying the Qassimi /aj aw/→[eː oː] monophthongization.

allophone_rules.notes: Qassimi monophthongization (Alhoody 2019:42, ex. /bajt/→[beːt] 'house'): the Classical diphthong /aj/ is realised [eː]. This is the defining vocalic delta from central Najdi (ar-SA-x-najd), which retains [aj]. Same tokenization caveat as the Hejazi HEJ_MONO_AY rule: the ⟨يَوْ⟩/⟨وَيْ⟩ spellings are handled by explicit graphemes because the onset digraphs يَ/وَ claim the fatha before an atom forms.
Qassimi monophthongization (Alhoody 2019:42, ex. /lawn/→[loːn] 'colour'): the Classical diphthong /aw/ is realised [oː]. Defining vocalic delta from central Najdi, which retains [aw].

location.notes: Buraydah, capital of the Qaṣīm region — representative point for Qassimi Arabic.

stress.notes: Quantity-sensitive Arabic stress, inherited from the Najdi parent; see ar-SA-x-najd for the full cascade and input contract. Prosodic clitics: monosyllabic function words (negator maː, prepositions fiː/min/ʕan/ʕa/li/bi, vocative jaː, copula-negator muː/miʃ, conditional law/loː, wa-proclitic combinations) attach to a host and fall inside the host's stress domain, so they carry no word stress (Watson 2002, The Phonology and Morphology of Arabic, OUP, ch.3). Pan-Arabic class; the per-lect form list reflects the function words attested in this lect. Orthographic test — a clitic homograph (e.g. verb yā 'came') is destressed in every occurrence.
